title: Vellum-covered boards, paring and lacing
subtitle: The classic medieval and Renaissance cover: taut calf vellum over binders board
excerpt: Vellum shrinks as it dries, pulling taut over the boards and creating a cover harder than cloth and more durable than paper. Paring the turn-ins to an even thinness, dampening the skin, and lacing the cords through the board are the three skills that make it work.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on covering in vellum. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Covers paring, dampening, and lacing vellum onto boards with worked examples.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of a covering material that wraps around the board edge and adheres to the inner face of the board. The turn-in is pared thin so the board edge shows a clean right angle.
Shaving the edge of leather or vellum progressively thinner using a paring knife, so that the turn-in lies flat without bulk.
The open edge of a bound book, opposite the spine, where the reader's thumb rests when opening.

content.text: Calf vellum, dampened and stretched over binders board, dries under tension. That tension is what makes a vellum cover so durable: the skin grips the board without adhesive and the surface hardens to a material that resists both moisture and abrasion. Getting it right requires accurate
paring

, controlled dampening, and lacing the covering cords through the board before the vellum goes on. Cockerell documented this approach in 1901 as the standard for high-quality covered boards; the method has not changed.

attrs.heading: Cutting tools safety
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path. Cut away from the body.

content.text: What you need

For the vellum: calfskin vellum is available from specialist bookbinding suppliers in full skins or pre-cut pieces. A piece 50-60 mm larger than the board on every side gives enough material for a full
turn-in

plus the lacing allowance. The vellum should be of even thickness and free from large holes. For the lacing: the covering cords (linen tape or thong) from the sewn text block pass through holes drilled in the board to anchor it.
Preparing the vellum piece

Cut the vellum to size on the cutting mat with a sharp craft knife, adding 50 mm turn-in allowance on each edge. Cut against a metal ruler; vellum is tough and the knife must be sharp and the cut decisive.

Pare the turn-in edges. Working on a glass or marble paring surface, shave the flesh side progressively thinner in the zone that will wrap around the board edge. The target thickness at the outermost 5 mm is roughly half the body thickness. The transition should be gradual, not stepped. A paring knife or a sharp craft knife held at a low angle both work; the stroke is a slicing draw rather than a push.
Dampen the flesh side of the vellum with a clean damp sponge. The vellum will go slightly limp as it absorbs moisture. Work quickly: you have 5-10 minutes before it dries under studio conditions.
Lacing the cords

Before applying vellum, lace the text-block cords through the board. Pierce pairs of holes through the board at each tape or cord position with the bookbinding awl: an entry hole near the inner face and an exit hole about 10 mm away on the same face. Thread the cord end through the entry hole, across the board between the two layers of board (if using a split board) or across the board surface, and out through the exit hole.
Fan the cord end across the board and paste it down, or hammer it flat with a bone folder so it does not create a ridge under the vellum.
Applying the vellum

Lay the dampened vellum flesh-side up on the mat. Centre the board on it, inner face down. Check the grain of the vellum runs parallel to the spine (like paper grain, the vellum should fold more easily in one direction; that direction is the spine direction).
Fold the spine turn-in over the board edge first. Burnish firmly with the bone folder, working the vellum into the corners. Then fold the
foredge
turn-in, pulling the vellum taut. Fold the head turn-in, then the tail.

At the corners, cut a mitred notch out of the vellum at 45 degrees, leaving a small amount of overlap, and fold the two sides down. Burnish the corner flat. Work quickly while the vellum remains damp.

Place the covered board under a clean board with weights and leave flat for at least 12 hours. As the vellum dries it contracts and grips the board. Do not force-dry with heat: rapid drying causes the vellum to blister or warp.
Common problems

items.symptom: Vellum blisters away from the board surface after drying
items.cause: The vellum was too dry when applied, or dried too quickly.
items.fix: Re-dampen the affected area with a barely damp sponge and re-press under weights. Prevention: dampen more generously and press immediately in a dry room.

items.cause: Vellum was applied to one face only without counterbalancing the pull.
items.fix: Cut a piece of dampened vellum or parchment for the inner face to balance the shrinkage. For a case binding, the endpaper paste serves this function.

items.cause: The corner mitres were not cut close enough or the paring was insufficient at the corner.
items.fix: Trim away any excess at the corner and burnish flat while the vellum is still damp. A lump of dried vellum at a corner is very difficult to correct after the fact.
